content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES

KRAUEL & KRAUEL
GOVERNOR PARK
5000 SHOREHAM PLACE, SUITE 101
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92122-5934

ROGER W. KRAUEL                                                (619) 455-9737
FRANCESCA MECIA KRAUEL
                                                               FAX: 455-1358


                              May 7, 1992


John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

    Re:  Request for Informal Assistance, I-92-119

Dear John:

    Thank you very much for your recent series of informal advice
letters. Based upon your input, I have produced the enclosed
memorandum which I intend to distribute to each of my clients. If
you have time, I would appreciate your reviewing my memorandum.

    I anticipate that you would find that the memorandum is consistent
with your advice. I thought you might be interested in seeing how
your work is utilized in the field.    Thank you again for your
assistance.

                                        Yours truly,


                                        ROGER W. KRAUEL
                                        City Attorney
                                        City of Coronado
                                        City of Del Mar
                                        City of Encinitas


RWK:mar

Enclosure

DATE:

TO: Mayor and the City Council
Cities of Coronado, Del Mar and Encinitas

FROM: City Attorney Roger W. Krauel

SUBJECT: Recent Informal Assistance from Fair Political Practices
Commission

In responses to a series of letters requesting informal assistance,
the Fair Political Practices Commission has presented several
analytical approaches to certain factual circumstances. I am
presenting these analytical approaches to you for information only.
When faced with a particular circumstance, my advice remains
unchanged: request an analysis by the FPPC of your particular
case.

Further, the question "What is the dollar amount of the effect of
the decision on my financial interest?" is neither a legal question
nor an FPPC question. It is a question for an appraiser or
financial consultant. The FPPC advises and the City Attorney
assists in answering "What test or tests apply to my circumstance?"

Finally, to properly review a potential decision for a conflict of
interest, the councilmember must consider the impact of the
decision on all types of economic interests, to include without
limitation the councilmember's business, real property, income and
gift interests.

Circumstance No. 1
REZONING PROPERTY:
EFFECT ON INTERESTS IN REAL PROPERTY

a.     The City Council is deciding whether to rezone land
       located in the City's R-1 zone.

b.     Councilmember X has a financial interest in real
       property located within the R-1 zone.

c.     Councilmember Y has a financial interest in real
       property that is in the R-2 zone and is 300 feet
       from the land that will be rezoned.

d.     Councilmember Z has a financial interest in real
       property that is in the R-2 zone and is more than
       300 feet from the land that will be rezoned.

Under what circumstances would each of the Councilmembers
be disqualified from participating in the decision to
amend the zoning ordinance?

Councilmember X

     1.     If the decision to rezone is not applicable to all
            property within the R-1 zone, then Councilmember X is
            disqualified if:

            a.     X's property is being rezoned; or

            b.     X's property is located within 300 feet of the land
                   being rezoned and it is reasonably foreseeable that
                   the decision would have any financial effect on X's
                   interest in real property; or

            c.     X's property is located more than 300 feet away from
                   the land being rezoned and it is reasonably
                   foreseeable that the financial effect of the
                   rezoning would be $10,000 or more on the fair
                   market value of the property in which X has an
                   interest, or would affect the rental value of such
                   property by $1,000 or more in a 12-month period
                   (collectively referred to hereinafter as the
                   "$10,000-$1,000 TEST".)

     2.     If the decision to rezone is applicable to all property
            within the R-1 zone category, then the $10,000-$1,000
            TEST is applied to determine whether Councilmember X is
            disqualified.

                                    2

Councilmember Y

1. With an interest in real property located within 300 feet
of the land being rezoned, Councilmember Y would likely
be disqualified from participation in the decision to
rezone if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
would have any financial effect on Y's interest in that
real property.

2. However, if X has an interest in real property located
within the R-1 zone and the $10,000-$1,000 TEST is
applicable to X because all the property in the R-1
category is subject to the rezone, the FPPC suggests
that the "any financial effect" standard would not be
applied to Y similar that would subject Y to a stricter
standard than X who owns property being rezoned.
Therefore, in such a situation, the FPPC suggests that
the $10,000 STANDARD is applied to both:

a. X whose property is being rezoned like all the
other property in the R-1 category; and

b. Y whose property is within 300 feet of the rezoning.

Councilmember Z

Since Councilmember Z has an interest in real property
located more than 300 feet away from the land subject to
the rezone, the $10,000-$1,000 TEST is applied to
determine whether Councilmember Z is disqualified.

Summary

Generally, a councilmember cannot participate in a decision to
rezone land that would:

1. Rezone property in which the councilmember has a
financial interest; or

2. Rezone property which is within 300 feet of property in
which the councilmember has a financial interest if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the rezone would have any
financial effect on the councilmember's financial
interest; or

3. Rezone property which is more that 300 feet away from
property in which the councilmember has a financial
interest if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
financial effect of the rezoning would be $10,000 or more
on the fair market value of the councilmember's interest

3

in real property, or would affect the rental value of
such property by $1,000 or more in a 12-month period.

However, if a councilmember has a financial interest in real
property in a zone and the entire zone is being rezoned in the same
manner, the $10,000-$1,000 TEST is applied to the real property
interest of that councilmember and also to other councilmembers.

4

Circumstance No. 2
"PUBLIC GENERALLY" EXCEPTION

a.     The City Council is making a decision.

b.     The decision will have a material financial effect
on an economic interest of Councilmember X.

c.     The decision will also effect a number of persons in
the public in the same manner that it will effect
the councilmember.

When does the "public generally" exception apply?

The FPPC regulations provide that even if a decision would have a
material financial effect on an economic interest of Councilmember
X, the Councilmember is not disqualified if the decision will
affect in substantially the same manner:

1.     The Councilmember's interest; and

2.     The interests of the public generally or a significant
segment of the public in the jurisdiction.

The FPPC acknowledges that it has never adopted a test for
determining what constitutes the "public generally" or a
"significant segment of the public." Therefore, there is no set
formula to determine when the exception applies.

The FPPC advises that a determination of whether the public
generally exception is applicable depends on the particular facts
of a specific decision.  I suggest that the "public generally"
exception should never be relied upon by a councilmember unless the
FPPC's formal advice has been sought and the FPPC advises that the
"public generally" exception does apply.

5

Circumstance No. 3
A DECISION CONCERNING A
PROJECT WITH ALTERNATIVES

a.     The City Council will decide where to place a public
       project.

b.     The project can be sited at alternatives 1, 2
       or 3.

c.     Councilmember X has a financial interest in real
       property that is located within 300 feet of
       alternative site 1.

Is Councilmember X disqualified from participating in the
decision regarding the project?

With a financial interest in real property located within 300 feet
of the site of Alternative 1, Councilmember X is disqualified from
the process if it is reasonably foreseeable that locating the
project on Alternative 1 will have any financial effect on X's
financial interests. So long as alternatives 1, 2 and 3 are being
discussed and the decision to select one would be a decision not to
select others, X is disqualified.

However, the City Council might follow a procedure such that
Councilmember X may not be disqualified from the entire decision
making process concerning the project. For example:

1.     The City Council could first address Alternative 1
       (without X's participation) and determine irrevocably not
       to place the project on Alternative 1. Thereafter, X
       might be able to participate in the process to select
       between Alternatives 2 and 3, so long as neither
       alternative 2 nor 3 would materially effect X's economic
       interests; or

2.     The City Council could first address Alternative 1
       (without X's participation) and determine to locate the
       project on Alternative 1. The City Council could then
       address a series of decisions implementing the project.
       Councilmember X could participate in any one of those
       subsequent decisions which did not have any financial
       effect on X's financial interest.


6

EXAMPLE

The City is considering a traffic circulation
plan and is required to select one of four
alternative routes (A-B-C-D) proposed for a
north-south collector; and to select one of
three alternative routes (E-F-G) proposed for
an east-west collector.

Councilmember X's real property is located within
300 feet of Alternative A for the north-south
collector.

The City Council's consideration of the traffic circulation plan
could proceed as follows:

1.  So long as all alternatives (A-B-C-D) for the north-south
    collector are being discussed and the decision to select
    one would be a decision not to select others, X could not
    participate.

2.  The City Council could consider alternative A in
    isolation and without X's participation.

3.  If the City Council selects alternative A for the north-
    south collector or absolutely and irrevocably rejects
    Alternative A, X can participate in each of the other
    decisions so long as X is not otherwise disqualified.

4.  Once the north-south collector and the east-west
    collector are designated by the City Council, X could
    cast a final vote to adopt or reject the entire traffic
    plan as a whole, and would not be disqualified so long as
    the traffic plan is not modified at that time of
    adoption.

Note:   If a decision concerning one segment of an overall plan
would decide or alter the other segment of the plan that presents
the councilmember with a conflict, the councilmember would be
disqualified as to both decisions. Alternatives may be too inter-
linked to be considered separately.

7

Circumstance No. 4
DETERMINING GEOGRAPHIC DISTANCE BETWEEN
THE COUNCILMEMBER'S REAL PROPERTY AND
THE SITE OF THE DECISION/PROJECT

a.    A square piece of property is developed already with
      three separate projects: one in the northeast quarter,
      one in the southwest quarter and one in the northwest
      corner.

b.    The southeast quarter is undeveloped.

c.    A separate project is proposed for the southeast quarter.

                        Councilmember property

                                 □


                                                    N
                                          ┌─────────────┐
                                          │             │
                            Other Property│             │
                                          └─────────────┘
                                              S


      Between which two points is distance measured to
      determine a disqualification concerning a Councilmember's
      interest in real property and the proposed project?

Generally, distance is measured from the property line of the
Councilmember's property to the property line of the other property
where the project is to be sited.

However, the FPPC suggests that where the other property is already
developed with separate projects and a new project is proposed for
an undeveloped portion of the other property, the measurement might
be made from the property line of the Councilmember's property to
where the proposed project is being constructed on the other
property, rather than the property line of the other property.

In this described circumstance, the distance could be measured from
the property line of the Councilmember's property to the
undeveloped, southeast quarter of the other property where the
proposed project is being considered.

If the decision concerning the new project in the southeast quarter
were inter-linked with a change concerning the structure in the
northwest quarter of the other property, the proposed project and
the change in the existing structure may each impact the
Councilmember's financial interest in real property.   In that
circumstance, the distance would be measured from both the
southeast quarter and the northwest quarter.


                                    8

sections.question_synthetic: This document is an attorney's memorandum to city councils summarizing FPPC informal advice on conflict of interest disqualification rules for councilmembers in various zoning and project decision scenarios.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is incoming correspondence from an attorney to FPPC counsel referencing prior informal advice letters (not the FPPC response itself). The document lacks formal Q&A structure; instead it contains a client memorandum summarizing FPPC guidance on multiple scenarios. Low confidence due to unclear whether this is the actual informal advice or the attorney's independent summary. Document appears truncated at end.

embedding.summary: A city attorney (Roger W. Kraiuel) forwards a memorandum to three California cities (Coronado, Del Mar, Encinitas) summarizing FPPC informal advice on conflict of interest disqualification standards for councilmembers, covering four specific circumstances: rezoning property, the public generally exception, project alternative site selection, and geographic distance measurement.
